d: Single-library approach: one Figma file contains all tokens, styles, and components. Best for small/medium teams with fewer than 5 designers. Advantages: simple dependency management, single publish action, easier onboarding. Disadvantages: file can become slow as it grows, harder to parallelize work, merge conflicts when multiple people edit. RULE: Keep files under 10,000 layers for performance. If you exceed this, split into multi-library.

Multi-library approach: separate Figma files for Foundations (tokens/variables), Core Components (atoms), Patterns/Templates (molecules/organisms), Icons, and Illustrations. Best for large teams with 5+ designers. Advantages: better performance per file, parallel editing, independent versioning, clear ownership. Disadvantages: more complex dependency management, update propagation requires accepting changes across files. Each library file should have a clear, singular purpose.

Recommended multi-library structure follows a dependency chain: Foundations file (variables, color/text/effect/grid styles) -> Core Components file (atoms: buttons, inputs, badges, icons) -> Patterns file (molecules/organisms: cards, forms, navigation bars, modals) -> Templates file (page layouts, screen compositions). Each level consumes the previous as an enabled library. Foundations has zero dependencies. Core depends on Foundations. Patterns depends on Core and Foundations. Templates depends on all.

File naming pattern: '[Team/Brand] - [Library Name]'. Examples: 'Design System - Foundations', 'Design System - Core Components', 'Design System - Patterns', 'Design System - Icons', 'Product - Marketing Templates'. Consistent naming helps team members find files quickly in Figma's file browser. Avoid generic names like 'Components' or 'Library' without team/brand prefix since multiple libraries may coexist in an organization.

RULE: Keep each Figma file under 10,000 layers for optimal performance. Signs of an oversized file: slow loading, laggy interactions, high memory usage, frequent crashes. Mitigation strategies: split into multi-library architecture, move deprecated components to a separate archive file, remove unused hidden layers, flatten complex illustrations into single images where possible. Monitor layer count via Figma's file stats or community plugins.

Recommended page order for library files: (1) Cover page with library name, version number, status badge, last updated date, team contact info. (2) Getting Started page with usage instructions, how to enable the library, changelog, contribution guidelines. (3) Component pages organized by category, one page per category (Buttons, Inputs, Cards, Navigation, etc.), in alphabetical order. (4) Playground/Sandbox page for testing work-in-progress components. (5) Deprecated page for components being phased out. RULE: Order pages logically — Cover first, Getting Started second, components alphabetical, utility pages last.

Use emoji or icon prefixes in page names for quick visual scanning in the layers panel. Common conventions: use a green circle for production-ready pages, a red circle for deprecated pages, a construction sign for work-in-progress, a book for documentation, a star for cover page. Examples: 'Cover', 'Getting Started', 'Buttons', 'Inputs', 'Deprecated', 'WIP / Playground'. Keep page names concise. The emoji prefix provides status at a glance without reading the full name.

Use Figma sections (Shift+S) to group related frames on a page. Create a section for each logical grouping: all variants of a component, interactive states, documentation frames, playground/testing area. Sections act as top-level canvas organizers. RULE: Sections are top-level canvas elements and should not be nested inside other sections. RULE: Name sections clearly and descriptively: 'Button - All Variants', 'Button - Usage Guidelines', 'Button - Interactive States'.

Color-code sections to communicate status at a glance. Recommended color scheme: green for ready/production components, yellow for components under review or needing feedback, red for deprecated or do-not-use components, blue for reference or documentation sections, gray/default for playground or testing areas. Apply section colors by selecting the section and choosing a color from the right panel. This convention should be documented on the Getting Started page so all team members interpret colors consistently.

RULE: Never leave default Figma names. Rename EVERYTHING. Bad names: 'Rectangle 1', 'Frame 17', 'Group 3', 'Vector 5'. Good names: descriptive nouns reflecting purpose — 'Primary Button', 'Search Input', 'User Avatar', 'Card Shadow', 'Icon Container'. Layer names should communicate what the element IS, not what shape it uses. Unnamed layers create confusion during handoff, make dev mode specs unreadable, and indicate sloppy design hygiene. Use plugins like 'Rename It' to batch-rename layers.

Components use PascalCase with slash (/) hierarchy for grouping in the assets panel. Pattern: 'Category/Subcategory/Variant'. Examples: 'Button/Primary/Large', 'Button/Primary/Small', 'Button/Secondary/Large', 'Input/Text/Default', 'Input/Text/Error', 'Icon/Navigation/ArrowLeft'. The slash creates nested folders in the assets panel. First segment = top-level group, subsequent segments = subgroups. RULE: Assets panel hierarchy equals component naming hierarchy — organize names carefully because renaming published components can break consumer files.

Variables use lowercase with slash or hyphen separators: 'color/primary/500', 'spacing/md', 'radius/lg', 'font-size/body', 'opacity/disabled'. Styles use Title Case with slash hierarchy: 'Colors/Primary/500', 'Typography/Body/Regular', 'Effects/Shadow/Medium', 'Grids/Desktop/12-Column'. RULE: Maintain consistent naming across the entire team. Document naming conventions in the style guide or Getting Started page. Inconsistent naming leads to duplicates and confusion.

Name frames and artboards with the pattern: 'Screen Name - State'. Examples: 'Login - Default', 'Login - Error', 'Login - Loading', 'Dashboard - Empty State', 'Dashboard - Populated', 'Settings - Profile Tab'. For responsive variants: 'Login - Default - Mobile', 'Login - Default - Desktop'. This convention makes prototype flows readable, dev handoff clear, and frame search (Ctrl/Cmd+F) effective. RULE: Consistent naming across team — document naming pattern and enforce in design reviews.

To publish a file as a library: open the file, go to the Assets panel (or main menu), click the book icon, then 'Publish'. Consumers enable the library: open any file, go to Assets panel, click the book icon, search for the library, toggle it on. Once enabled, all published components and styles from that library appear in the consumer's assets panel. Updates to the library require consumers to accept changes via a notification in the assets panel. RULE: Publish with descriptive update notes so consumers understand what changed.

Library dependency chain must be one-directional: Foundations -> Core -> Patterns -> Templates. RULE: NEVER create circular dependencies. If Core depends on Foundations, Foundations must never depend on Core. Circular dependencies cause infinite update loops and unpredictable behavior. Update propagation follows the chain: Foundations update -> Core must accept changes -> Patterns must accept changes -> Templates must accept changes. RULE: Minimize cross-library dependencies to keep coupling loose. A component in Core should not reference a component in Patterns.

Version libraries independently so each can evolve at its own pace. Example: Foundations v2.1, Core Components v3.0, Patterns v1.5. Use the cover page and Getting Started page to display the current version number. Name important Figma versions (Ctrl/Cmd+Alt+S) at each release: 'v2.1 - Added new spacing scale'. Publish notes should reference the version. This allows teams to track which version of each library they are consuming and plan updates accordingly.

Group components by functional category using slash naming: Navigation/ (Navbar, Tabs, Breadcrumb, Sidebar), Forms/ (Input, Select, Checkbox, Radio, Toggle, DatePicker), Feedback/ (Alert, Toast, Modal, Dialog, Snackbar), Data Display/ (Table, Card, Badge, Avatar, Tag, List), Layout/ (Container, Grid, Divider, Spacer, Stack), Overlay/ (Dropdown, Tooltip, Popover, Menu). This grouping maps directly to how developers think about UI and makes the assets panel intuitive to browse. Within each group, order components alphabetically.

Alternative grouping by atomic design level: Atoms/ (Button, Badge, Icon, Avatar, Divider, Tag — smallest indivisible elements), Molecules/ (Card, ListItem, FormField, SearchBar, MenuItem — combinations of atoms), Organisms/ (Header, Sidebar, Modal, DataTable, Form, Footer — complex UI sections composed of molecules). This approach emphasizes composition hierarchy. Choose either functional or atomic grouping — do not mix both in the same library as it creates confusion about where to find components.

Hide internal or helper components that should not be used directly by consumers. Methods: (1) Right-click the component > Move to a dedicated internal/private page, then right-click > 'Hide from publishing' in the assets panel. (2) Prefix component names with '_' or '.' (e.g., '_BaseButton', '.IconWrapper') as a naming convention signaling internal use. Hidden components still function as building blocks for published components but do not clutter the consumer's assets panel. RULE: Do not mix published and unpublished components on the same page.

Design debt accumulates when shortcuts are taken. Key debt indicators: (1) Detached instances — components that were detached and should be reconnected. Audit regularly using plugins or manual search. (2) Unnamed layers — default names like 'Rectangle 1' or 'Frame 42'. Use batch-rename plugins. (3) Hardcoded values — colors, sizes, or spacing that should reference variables or styles but use raw values instead. (4) Unused components — components with zero instances across all files. (5) Duplicate components — multiple components serving the same purpose. RULE: Schedule quarterly design debt reviews to keep libraries healthy.

Track design debt with measurable metrics: detached instances count (target: zero in production files), unnamed layers count (target: zero), hardcoded color values count (target: zero — all should use variables), hardcoded spacing values count, unused published components count, duplicate component pairs count. Use Figma plugins like 'Design Lint' or 'Stark' to automate audits. Report metrics monthly to stakeholders. RULE: Define acceptable thresholds and escalate when debt exceeds them. Design debt slows down teams and degrades consistency over time.

Handoff preparation checklist: (1) All layers properly named with descriptive names. (2) All components using variables and styles — no hardcoded values. (3) Auto-layout applied for accurate spacing specs in dev mode. (4) Developer annotations added for complex interactions, edge cases, or non-obvious behaviors. (5) Prototype flows linked for interactive behavior reference. (6) Component descriptions filled with usage guidelines and prop documentation. RULE: Use sections labeled 'Ready for Dev' to indicate handoff-ready designs. RULE: Never hand off designs with unnamed layers or detached instances.

Figma branching workflow: Create branch (Edit > Create branch or from file menu) for isolated changes. Branch naming conventions: 'feature/[name]' for new components, 'fix/[name]' for bug fixes, 'experiment/[name]' for explorations. Work on the branch without affecting main. Share the branch URL for team review and feedback. When approved, merge back to main: compare changes, resolve conflicts (Figma highlights differences), complete merge. RULE: Branch for new components, breaking changes, and major redesigns. RULE: Do not branch for small fixes, text changes, or minor color tweaks — edit main directly.

Figma auto-saves all changes to version history. Name important versions explicitly with Ctrl/Cmd+Alt+S. Naming conventions: 'v2.1 - Added toggle component', 'v3.0 - Breaking: new spacing scale', 'Pre-refactor snapshot', 'Approved by stakeholders 2024-03-15'. Named versions are easy to find and restore. Use named versions before major refactors as safety snapshots. Version history retention depends on Figma plan. RULE: Always name a version before merging a branch so you can revert if the merge introduces issues.

Design system contribution process: (1) Propose — open an issue or request describing the new component or change. (2) Design — create a Figma branch with the implementation. (3) Review — share branch with team for feedback, iterate on comments. (4) Approve — system maintainer reviews for consistency, naming, documentation completeness. (5) Publish — merge branch to main, publish library update with descriptive notes. Roles: System Owner makes architecture decisions, Maintainers review and merge contributions, Contributors propose and design. RULE: No component ships without documentation. RULE: No breaking change without a migration guide. RULE: All published changes require at least one review.

Breaking changes require special handling: announce deprecation at least one release in advance, provide a migration guide explaining how to update from old to new, mark deprecated components visually (red section, strikethrough in name, 'DEPRECATED' prefix). Communication cadence: publish a changelog with every library update, send notifications via Slack or Teams for significant changes, hold a monthly design system sync meeting for roadmap and Q&A. RULE: Never remove a component without a deprecation period. Consumers need time to migrate.

Standard component documentation layout within Figma: (1) Title and description at the top. (2) Component preview showing the default state. (3) Properties table listing all variants, props, and their values. (4) Usage guidelines explaining when and how to use the component. (5) Do/Don't examples side by side with green checkmark for correct usage and red X for incorrect. (6) Related components section linking to similar or complementary components. (7) Code snippet in the component description for developer reference. RULE: Documentation lives next to the component in Figma — not exclusively in a separate tool. Figma is the single source of truth.
